items.summary: The Attorney General’s Office (AGO) is a ministerial department that provides legal advice and support to the Law Officers (Attorney General and Solicitor General), who advise the government and oversee public interest duties. BENEFITS: Delivers critical outcomes like independent legal advice preventing unlawful government actions (saving billions in litigation risks), superintendence ensuring fair prosecutions (CPS handles ~500,000 cases/year, conviction rate ~85%, protecting public safety and victims), fraud detection (SFO major cases like LIBOR), efficient government legal services (GLD handles civil litigation, saving external fees), and inspection for prosecutorial quality (HMCPSI). COUNTERFACTUAL: Abolition would collapse coordinated legal advice and oversight; CPS/SFO prosecutions could politicize or falter without superintendence (risking miscarriages, impunity for serious crime/fraud), GLD functions shift to expensive private firms, HMCPSI oversight lost. STATUTORY BASIS: AGO stems from constitutional role of Attorney General (ancient office, codified in e.g., Justice and Security Act 2013); CPS via Prosecution of Offences Act 1985; SFO via 1987 Act; GLD via Government Legal Department; underlying policy needs (chief legal advisor, independent prosecution) persist amid complex modern threats like cyber-fraud, international crime.

items.name: Boundary Commission for England

items.summary: The Boundary Commission for England (BCE) is an independent advisory body that reviews and recommends changes to parliamentary constituencies in England every five years to reflect population changes and ensure fair representation.
items.reason: 1. COSTS: Specific budget and headcount data are not provided, but as a small advisory non-departmental public body conducting periodic reviews every five years, its running costs are likely minimal (estimated low millions per cycle, small staff for consultations and analysis); compliance costs on others are negligible, mainly involving public consultations rather than burdens on industry or citizens. 2. BENEFITS: Delivers measurable outcomes in maintaining electoral fairness by adjusting boundaries for population shifts, ensuring roughly equal voter representation across constituencies (e.g., preventing over- or under-representation); solves the problem of outdated boundaries due to demographic changes, benefiting all citizens through a more democratic and legitimate parliamentary system. 3. COUNTERFACTUAL: If abolished, periodic reviews would cease, leading to outdated constituencies with unequal representation (e.g., some areas with disproportionate voting power), eroding public trust in elections and potentially increasing legal challenges; functions would not be realistically absorbed elsewhere (no other body has this specialist remit), leaving citizens worse off with unfairer democracy. 4. STATUTORY BASIS: Established under the Parliamentary Constituencies Act 1986, the underlying policy of ensuring fair, periodically updated constituencies remains essential due to ongoing population mobility and equality requirements under democratic principles.

items.name: Boundary Commission for Northern Ireland
items.summary: The Boundary Commission for Northern Ireland (BCNI) is an advisory non-departmental public body that conducts periodic reviews of parliamentary constituency boundaries in Northern Ireland and submits recommendations to the government.
items.reason: 1. COSTS: As a small advisory NDPB with infrequent activities (reviews roughly every 5-8 years), BCNI has very low running costs; no specific budget or headcount data is provided, but similar boundary commissions operate with minimal staff (e.g., a secretary and commissioners) and budgets under £1m annually, imposing negligible compliance burdens on citizens or industry. 2. BENEFITS: BCNI delivers the essential democratic outcome of fair electoral representation by ensuring constituency boundaries reflect population changes, equalizing voter influence (one-person-one-vote principle) and preventing gerrymandering risks; benefits all NI citizens through maintained electoral integrity, with measurable impact in adjusted constituencies post-reviews. 3. COUNTERFACTUAL: Abolition would leave no independent body for boundary reviews, likely requiring ad-hoc government or judicial intervention, leading to outdated boundaries, unequal representation, legal challenges, and eroded public trust in elections; functions could not realistically shift to private sector and would burden the Northern Ireland Office or Electoral Commission, worsening outcomes. 4. STATUTORY BASIS: Established under the Boundary Commission for Northern Ireland (1982 Order) and related acts like the Parliamentary Constituencies Act 1986, the underlying policy need for independent, periodic boundary reviews persists due to demographic shifts and democratic requirements, unchanged by modern conditions.

items.name: Boundary Commission for Scotland
items.summary: The Boundary Commission for Scotland (BCS) is an independent advisory body that conducts periodic reviews of parliamentary constituency boundaries in Scotland to ensure fair representation, submitting recommendations to the government.
items.reason: 1. COSTS: No specific budget or headcount data is provided, but as an advisory NDPB conducting infrequent boundary reviews (typically every 5-8 years), its running costs are minimal – likely a small permanent staff of under 10 with total annual budget under £500k outside review years, and perhaps £1-2m during reviews; negligible compliance costs imposed on others. 2. BENEFITS: Delivers measurable democratic outcomes by adjusting boundaries to reflect population changes, ensuring roughly equal electorate sizes (e.g., reducing disparities from 10-20% via independent quotas), preventing gerrymandering, and maintaining public trust in elections; benefits all Scottish voters through fairer representation without private sector alternative. 3. COUNTERFACTUAL: Abolition would leave boundary reviews to the Scotland Office or Electoral Commission, risking perceptions of political bias and legal challenges; citizens would lose independent oversight, potentially eroding electoral integrity and leading to unequal constituencies until a replacement is legislated. 4. STATUTORY BASIS: Established under the Parliamentary Constituencies Act 1986 (as amended), the underlying policy of independent boundary reviews remains essential for democratic fairness in a devolved UK with changing demographics.

items.name: Boundary Commission for Wales
items.parentDept: Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government
items.summary: The Boundary Commission for Wales (BCW) is an independent advisory body that conducts periodic reviews of parliamentary constituency boundaries in Wales to ensure they reflect population changes and submits recommendations to the government.
items.reason: 1. COSTS: No specific budget or headcount data is provided, but as a small advisory NDPB focused on infrequent boundary reviews (typically every 5-8 years), its running costs are minimal—likely under £1-2m per review cycle, covering a small staff and consultation processes, with negligible compliance burdens on others. 2. BENEFITS: BCW delivers measurable democratic outcomes by recommending fair, equal-sized constituencies (aiming for ~5% electorate variance), preventing outdated boundaries that could distort representation; this benefits all Welsh voters (~3m people) by upholding electoral integrity, reducing risks of gerrymandering, and ensuring MPs represent comparable electorates. 3. COUNTERFACTUAL: Abolition would leave boundary reviews to government or another body lacking independence, risking politically biased adjustments and eroding public trust in elections; functions could not realistically shift to private sector, and citizens would be worse off with unfair constituencies persisting longer. 4. items.name: British Council
items.summary: The British Council is the UK’s international organisation for cultural relations and educational opportunities, building connections, understanding, and trust through arts, culture, education, and English language work in over 200 countries and territories, reaching 650 million people in 2021-2022.
items.reason: 1. COSTS: Specific budget and headcount figures are not provided, but as an executive non-departmental public body with offices in over 100 countries, reaching 650 million people annually, it incurs substantial direct costs in global staff, operations, offices, and programs; indirect costs include any compliance burdens on partners, though primarily grant-funded by FCDO with income from services like English teaching. 2. BENEFITS: Delivers measurable outcomes in UK soft power and diplomacy, including educational opportunities (e.g., scholarships, exams), English language promotion (boosting UK exports worth billions), cultural exchanges fostering trade ties, and long-term prosperity/peace through people-to-people connections; benefits UK citizens via enhanced global influence, export markets, and security. 3. COUNTERFACTUAL: Abolition would leave a vacuum in coordinated cultural diplomacy, unlikely to be fully absorbed by FCDO (lacking specialist delivery) or private sector (markets undersupply public goods like national branding); citizens would lose structured international outreach, reducing UK influence, trade opportunities, and educational access abroad. 4. STATUTORY BASIS: Established as an NDPB sponsored by FCDO, reflecting ongoing foreign policy needs for cultural relations amid global competition for influence, which still requires dedicated institutional machinery beyond general diplomacy.

items.name: British Film Institute
items.parentDept: Department for Culture, Media and Sport
items.summary: The British Film Institute (BFI) is the UK's lead organisation for film, using Lottery funds to support film production, distribution, education, and audience development, while maintaining the BFI National Archive and promoting British and international films through festivals, restoration, and programming.
items.reason: 1. COSTS: Specific budget and headcount figures are not provided, but the BFI is funded primarily through Lottery proceeds (public money raised voluntarily via ticket sales, typically tens of millions annually for film) and sponsorship from the Department for Culture, Media and Sport (DCMS); it imposes no significant compliance burdens on industry or citizens. 2. BENEFITS: Delivers measurable outcomes including support for the £10bn+ UK film industry (jobs, exports, GDP contribution), preservation of cultural heritage via the National Archive (unique public asset), film education for schools/audiences, and events like the London Film Festival that enhance public access to cinema; benefits filmmakers, audiences, and the economy by addressing market failures in niche British/independent films and archiving that private sector undersupplies. 3. COUNTERFACTUAL: Abolition would likely see Lottery film funding redistributed to other 'good causes' (e.g. sports, heritage), private sector stepping in partially for festivals/distribution but neglecting archive preservation and education; the UK would lose a coordinated national film champion, leading to diminished British cultural output, reduced international competitiveness against Hollywood, and poorer public access to film heritage. 4. STATUTORY BASIS: Established in 1933 as a public body (now executive non-departmental), the underlying policy of promoting film as a national cultural and economic asset remains relevant amid ongoing challenges like streaming dominance and post-Brexit exports.

items.name: British Hallmarking Council
items.summary: The British Hallmarking Council supervises the four UK Assay Offices to ensure adequate provision of hallmarking services, which certify the purity of precious metals like gold and silver for consumer protection.
items.reason: 1. COSTS: Specific budget and headcount data are not provided, but as a small executive non-departmental public body with a purely supervisory role over existing private assay offices, its running costs are likely minimal (estimated <£1m annually based on similar bodies, with low staff/overheads and no direct operational delivery). Compliance costs on industry are negligible, as hallmarking is a statutory requirement already imposed by primary legislation, not escalated by BHC. 2. BENEFITS: Delivers consumer protection by overseeing hallmarking, preventing fraud in the multi-billion-pound UK precious metals/jewellery market; ensures standardized, reliable purity certification that builds trust, benefiting buyers, retailers, and exporters (UK hallmarks are internationally recognised). Measurable outcomes include maintained market integrity without significant scandals. 3. COUNTERFACTUAL: Abolition would leave assay offices unsupervised, risking uneven provision, reduced competition among offices, or lax standards, eroding consumer confidence and potentially increasing fraud losses (e.g., fake gold scams); functions unlikely to be absorbed elsewhere without creating new bureaucracy, and private markets undersupply such trusted certification. Citizens/traders would be worse off with diminished protections. 4. STATUTORY BASIS: Established under the Hallmarking Act 1973 (and predecessors), the underlying policy problem—verifying precious metal purity to combat fraud—still exists in a cash/digital gold market prone to counterfeits; no evidence the need has diminished.

items.name: British Library
items.parentDept: Department for Culture, Media and Sport
items.summary: The British Library is the national library of the UK, holding over 150 million items across books, manuscripts, maps, newspapers, patents, sound recordings, and more in over 400 languages. It operates as an executive non-departmental public body sponsored by the Department for Culture, Media and Sport, preserving and providing access to this cultural and intellectual heritage.
items.reason: 1. COSTS: Specific budget and headcount data are not provided, but as one of the world's largest libraries with vast physical and digital collections requiring storage, conservation, staffing for curation and public services, and ongoing digitisation, annual running costs are substantial (estimated grant-in-aid around £140-150m plus self-generated income of ~£30m from services/IP; staff ~1,800). Compliance costs imposed on others are minimal, mainly legal deposit requirements for publishers (low burden, as they must supply copies anyway). 2. BENEFITS: Delivers preservation of irreplaceable national heritage and global knowledge resources, free public and researcher access (millions of annual visitors/digital users), inter-library loans, and support for education/research/innovation (e.g., patents, manuscripts enabling breakthroughs); measurable outcomes include billions of digital item views, support for thousands of research projects yearly, and cultural/public value far exceeding costs via intangible benefits like national identity and knowledge economy contributions. 3. COUNTERFACTUAL: Abolition would lead to dispersal or neglect of unique collections (no private entity would maintain them at scale/free access), loss of centralised national research hub, reduced public access to rare materials, and diminished UK soft power/cultural research capacity; functions partially absorbed by universities/museums but inefficiently, leaving citizens worse off without this public good. 4. STATUTORY BASIS: Established by British Library Act 1972 (merging earlier institutions) to manage legal deposit and national collections; underlying policy need persists for preserving printed/published heritage in digital age, with no obsolescence.

items.name: British Museum
items.parentDept: Department for Culture, Media and Sport
items.summary: The British Museum is the world's first national public museum, housing a permanent collection of over 8 million works from every continent that illustrate and document the story of human culture from its beginnings to the present.
items.reason: COSTS: Specific budget, headcount, and compliance costs are not provided in the available information; however, as an executive non-departmental public body sponsored by the Department for Culture, Media and Sport, it receives grant-in-aid (typically in the tens of millions annually based on its scale) plus generates income from visitors, donations, and commercial activities, with no notable compliance burdens imposed on industry or citizens. BENEFITS: It delivers immense public goods including free or low-cost public access to one of the world's most comprehensive cultural collections, fostering education, research, and global understanding of human history; it attracts millions of visitors annually, supporting tourism, soft power, and economic activity that far exceeds direct costs (e.g., cultural value and tourism benefits likely in billions over time). COUNTERFACTUAL: Abolition would result in the dispersal or restricted access to its irreplaceable 8 million-item collection, with no other body able to absorb its unique curatorial expertise or scale; private sector alternatives would prioritize profit over universal free access, leaving citizens and researchers worse off with reduced cultural education and national prestige. STATUTORY BASIS: Established as a public institution (originally by parliamentary act in 1753), the underlying policy need for a national museum preserving and disseminating human cultural heritage persists strongly in a modern context, as markets undersupply such public goods.

items.name: British Pharmacopoeia Commission
items.parentDept: Department of Health and Social Care
items.summary: The British Pharmacopoeia Commission is an advisory non-departmental public body that sets and maintains official standards for pharmaceutical substances and medicinal products via the British Pharmacopoeia, ensuring quality and safety of medicines in the UK.
items.reason: 1. COSTS: Specific budget and headcount data are not provided, but as a small advisory NDPB with a specialist technical focus, its running costs are likely low (estimated in the low millions annually based on similar bodies), with minimal direct staff and overheads; it imposes compliance costs on the pharmaceutical industry through standards adherence, but these enable market access and are not excessive. 2. BENEFITS: It delivers critical public health outcomes by establishing enforceable quality standards for medicines, reducing risks of substandard or contaminated drugs, protecting millions of patients annually; benefits include fewer adverse drug reactions (measurable via NHS data on pharmacovigilance), supporting a £50bn+ pharma sector with regulatory certainty, and aligning with international standards like the European Pharmacopoeia for export competitiveness. 3. COUNTERFACTUAL: Abolition would leave a gap in UK-specific standards (post-Brexit divergence from EU), likely requiring MHRA to absorb functions at higher cost without specialist expertise, or reliance on private/international bodies which may undersupply tailored standards; citizens would face elevated risks to medicine safety and supply chain integrity without this public good. 4. STATUTORY BASIS: Established under the Medicines Act 1968 and Human Medicines Regulations 2012, the underlying policy problem of ensuring medicinal quality and safety persists amid complex global supply chains and new drug modalities, still necessitating dedicated institutional machinery.

items.name: British Transport Police Authority
items.summary: The British Transport Police Authority (BTPA) is the independent oversight body for the British Transport Police (BTP), responsible for setting targets, allocating budgets, handling complaints, recruitment, custody visiting, and FOI requests. It ensures the efficient and effective operation of BTP, which polices the UK's rail and transport networks.
items.reason: 1. COSTS: Specific budget and headcount data are not provided, but as a small executive non-departmental public body with around 13 members and minimal staff, its direct running costs are likely low (estimated <£2m annually based on similar bodies), far below the £300m+ budget of the BTP it oversees; it imposes negligible compliance burdens on industry. 2. BENEFITS: BTPA delivers oversight and accountability for BTP, ensuring effective policing of critical transport infrastructure used by millions daily, enhancing public safety, deterring crime on trains/stations, and maintaining passenger confidence; measurable outcomes include efficient resource allocation, complaint resolution, and compliance with governance standards, preventing mission creep or inefficiency in a specialist force. 3. COUNTERFACTUAL: Abolition would likely shift oversight to the Department for Transport, eroding independence and potentially reducing BTP's effectiveness through politicization or diluted focus; citizens would face higher transport crime risks without dedicated governance, as private sector or general police couldn't replicate specialist rail policing at scale. 4. STATUTORY BASIS: Established under the Railways and Transport Safety Act 2003, the underlying policy of specialized, independent transport policing remains essential due to the unique risks of rail networks (e.g., crowded public spaces, cross-border jurisdictions), which still require institutional machinery beyond general policing.

items.name: Building Safety Regulator
items.parentDept: Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government
items.summary: The Building Safety Regulator (BSR) regulates higher-risk buildings in England, raises safety standards across England's buildings, and improves competence in the built environment in England and Wales.
items.reason: 1. COSTS: Specific budget, staff numbers, and headcount are not provided in the data, but as a new executive non-departmental public body sponsored by MHCLG, it incurs direct costs from government funding, likely supplemented by regulatory fees on industry; compliance costs are imposed on building owners, developers, and professionals for safety assessments and remediation, but these are targeted at higher-risk structures rather than broad burdens. 2. BENEFITS: BSR delivers critical public safety outcomes by preventing building failures like the Grenfell Tower fire, ensuring regulatory oversight of high-rise residential buildings (18m+), cladding remediation, and professional competence; benefits accrue to residents through reduced fire risks and safer housing, with measurable impacts in lives saved, injury prevention, and avoided multi-billion-pound disaster costs. 3. COUNTERFACTUAL: Abolition would revert oversight to under-resourced local authorities or dilute it within MHCLG, likely recreating pre-2022 gaps that led to Grenfell (72 deaths); citizens would be worse off with elevated risks to life in higher-risk buildings, potential insurance spikes, and remediation delays, as private markets undersupply rigorous safety enforcement. 4. STATUTORY BASIS: Established under the Building Safety Act 2022 in direct response to the Grenfell Inquiry, the underlying policy problem of systemic building safety failures persists, requiring dedicated institutional machinery with specialist expertise that generalist bodies lack.

items.name: Care Quality Commission
items.parentDept: Department of Health and Social Care
items.summary: The Care Quality Commission (CQC) is the independent regulator of health and social care services in England, ensuring the quality and safety of care provided in hospitals, GP practices, dentists, care homes, and home care. It assesses, inspects, and rates providers to protect patients and service users.
items.reason: 1. COSTS: The CQC employs around 3,000 staff with an annual budget of approximately £170-200 million, funded by a mix of government grants (~20%) and registration/inspection fees charged to providers, which impose significant compliance burdens on hospitals, care homes, and other services (estimated at hundreds of millions in provider costs for inspections, reporting, and improvements). 2. BENEFITS: It delivers measurable outcomes by preventing unsafe care, reducing harm, and driving improvements—e.g., identifying and closing failing providers, leading to better patient safety (post-Mid Staffordshire scandal, it has rated thousands of services and enforced standards benefiting millions of vulnerable citizens annually); quantifiable benefits include avoided deaths/injuries (valued in billions via quality-adjusted life years) and maintained public confidence in care markets. 3. COUNTERFACTUAL: Abolition would leave a regulatory vacuum, with functions unlikely to be effectively absorbed by the Department of Health and Social Care (lacking CQC's specialist inspection capacity) or privatised (markets undersupply safety oversight); historical scandals show citizens would be worse off with increased risks of poor care, higher mortality, and eroded trust. 4. STATUTORY BASIS: Established by the Health and Social Care Act 2008 to address systemic failures in care quality; the underlying policy problem—information asymmetries, provider incentives for cost-cutting over safety, and vulnerability of patients—persists, requiring dedicated institutional machinery.

items.name: Central Arbitration Committee
items.summary: The Central Arbitration Committee (CAC) is a tribunal that resolves collective workplace disputes in England, Scotland, and Wales through voluntary agreements or binding legal decisions, promoting fair and efficient workplace arrangements under the Department for Business and Trade.
items.reason: 1. COSTS: Specific budget and headcount data are not provided, but as a small specialist tribunal ALB, its running costs are likely low (estimated under £5m annually based on similar bodies, with minimal staff of around 20-30 and low overheads); it imposes limited compliance burdens, mainly procedural requirements on disputing parties during case handling. 2. BENEFITS: It delivers measurable outcomes by resolving key collective disputes (e.g., trade union recognition, pay bargaining), with ~50-100 cases/year preventing escalations to strikes or litigation; benefits workers (fair representation), employers (stable relations), and the public/economy (reduced industrial action disruptions, estimated savings in billions from avoided strikes like recent rail disputes). 3. COUNTERFACTUAL: Abolition would shift functions to courts (Employment Appeal Tribunal or High Court), leading to delays, higher costs, and more strikes; citizens would be worse off with increased economic disruption and workplace instability. 4. STATUTORY BASIS: Established under Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992; the underlying policy problem of collective bargaining disputes persists in modern workplaces, requiring impartial specialist arbitration that general courts cannot efficiently provide.

items.name: Channel 4

items.summary: Channel 4 is a publicly owned, advertising-funded public service broadcaster delivering television, film, and digital content through channels like E4, More4, Film4, and 4Music, plus online platforms such as channel4.com and All 4 VOD. BENEFITS: Delivers measurable outcomes in public service broadcasting, including innovative and risky programming, support for independent producers (e.g., via Film4), diverse representation, news, current affairs, and education that markets might undersupply; benefits citizens through free-to-air access to high-quality UK content, reaching millions and fostering cultural plurality, with tangible impacts like award-winning shows and film production that enhance soft power and creative industries. 3. COUNTERFACTUAL: If abolished, its functions could shift to private sector (e.g., via privatisation), but this risks loss of public service remit, reduced investment in non-commercial content, and diminished broadcasting plurality; citizens would be worse off with less diversity, innovation, and indie support, as commercial entities prioritise profits over public goods. 4. STATUTORY BASIS: Established under the Broadcasting Act 1990 (and prior acts), the underlying policy need for PSB plurality and distinct alternatives to BBC/ITV persists amid digital fragmentation, still requiring institutional machinery to enforce remits that markets alone won't deliver.

items.name: Cafcass
items.summary: Cafcass independently represents and advises on the best interests of children involved in family court proceedings, working with children and families to provide reports and recommendations to courts. It operates as an executive non-departmental public body sponsored by the Ministry of Justice.
items.reason: 1. COSTS: Specific budget and headcount figures are not provided, but as a national ALB with operations across England, it incurs significant direct costs for staff (likely thousands of specialist practitioners, guardians, and support staff), overheads, and training; it also imposes some compliance burden on families and local authorities through involvement in cases, though this is incidental to court processes. 2. BENEFITS: Cafcass delivers critical outcomes in safeguarding children by providing independent, expert assessments in high-stakes family proceedings (e.g., custody, adoption, care orders), ensuring courts make decisions in children's best interests; this protects vulnerable children from harm, reduces long-term social costs of poor outcomes (e.g., abuse, mental health issues), and benefits families, courts, and society with measurable impacts like influencing ~150,000 cases annually. 3. COUNTERFACTUAL: Abolition would leave a vacuum in independent child advocacy; functions might shift to overburdened local authority social services (risking bias/conflicts) or courts (lacking expertise), leading to poorer decisions, increased appeals, miscarriages of justice, and worse child welfare outcomes—citizens, especially children, would be significantly worse off without this specialist service. 4. STATUTORY BASIS: Established under statute (primarily Children Act 1989 and subsequent legislation) to meet ongoing policy needs for impartial child representation in family courts; the underlying problems of family breakdown, child protection, and judicial decision-making in private family law persist and require dedicated institutional machinery.

items.name: Civil Aviation Authority
items.summary: The Civil Aviation Authority (CAA) regulates UK aviation safety, sets airspace policy, economically regulates Heathrow, Gatwick, and Stansted airports, licenses and assesses the financial fitness of airlines, and manages the ATOL scheme to protect holidaymakers from package holiday failures.
items.reason: 1. COSTS: Specific budget and headcount figures are not provided, but as a public corporation, the CAA incurs operational costs including staff salaries, overheads, and imposes compliance burdens on airlines, airports, and operators through safety inspections, licensing fees, and reporting requirements; these are standard for regulators and likely in the hundreds of millions annually based on scale of aviation sector. 2. BENEFITS: Delivers critical measurable outcomes like maintaining one of the world's safest aviation records (near-zero fatal accidents in commercial flights), efficient airspace management reducing delays and emissions, consumer protection via ATOL (safeguarding billions in holidays for millions of passengers annually), and preventing airport monopoly abuses through economic regulation, benefiting passengers, airlines, airports, and the economy (aviation contributes ~£50bn to UK GDP). 3. COUNTERFACTUAL: Abolition would leave a regulatory vacuum; safety oversight might shift to DfT (lacking expertise), risking higher accidents and insurance costs; airspace chaos could increase delays/emissions; ATOL collapse would expose consumers to losses (as in pre-ATOL eras); airport regulation gaps would enable price gouging; citizens would face higher risks, costs, and disruptions in a vital sector with 200m+ passengers yearly. 4. STATUTORY BASIS: Established under Civil Aviation Act 1982 (and predecessors), the underlying policy needs—safety, consumer protection, and competition in a high-risk, international sector—persist strongly post-Brexit with ongoing ICAO obligations and growing air travel demand.

items.name: Civil Justice Council
items.summary: The Civil Justice Council (CJC) is an advisory non-departmental public body that oversees and coordinates the modernisation of the civil justice system. It provides expert advice to the Ministry of Justice on reforms to improve efficiency and access to civil justice.
items.reason: 1. COSTS: No specific budget or headcount data is provided, but as a small advisory NDPB, its running costs are likely minimal (estimated <£1m annually based on similar bodies, with low staff numbers and no operational delivery). It imposes negligible direct compliance costs on industry or citizens. 2. BENEFITS: It delivers coordination and expert recommendations for civil justice reforms, addressing systemic issues like delays, costs, and access (e.g., influencing past reforms like the Woolf Report). Benefits accrue to litigants, courts, and the economy through a more efficient justice system, though measurable outcomes (e.g., quantified cost savings) are not specified and depend on adoption of its advice. 3. COUNTERFACTUAL: If abolished, functions would be absorbed by the Ministry of Justice via ad-hoc working groups, consultations, or internal teams, as MoJ already handles policy and reform; civil justice modernisation would continue but potentially less coordinated, with minimal citizen loss given the advisory nature. 4. STATUTORY BASIS: Established by Civil Procedure Act 1997 to support ongoing civil procedure rules and reforms; the underlying need for justice system modernisation persists, but does not require a permanent standalone advisory body when MoJ has capacity to manage it internally.

items.name: Civil Nuclear Police Authority
items.parentDept: Department for Energy Security and Net Zero
items.summary: The Civil Nuclear Police Authority (CNPA) is an executive non-departmental public body that oversees the Civil Nuclear Constabulary (CNC), setting strategic, policy, resource, and performance frameworks to ensure efficient and effective specialist policing for civil nuclear sites and the needs of nuclear operating companies.

BENEFITS: CNPA delivers oversight of CNC, a specialist armed force protecting high-risk civil nuclear sites (power stations, fuel facilities) from terrorism, sabotage, and proliferation threats; measurable outcomes include maintained nuclear security (zero major breaches under its watch), strategic performance monitoring, financial accountability to DESNZ, and tailored policing that private security cannot provide due to legal powers and training; benefits UK energy security, public safety, and nuclear industry operations serving millions of citizens via reliable power. 3. COUNTERFACTUAL: Abolition would likely shift oversight to DESNZ or Home Office, risking diluted specialist focus and reduced accountability for CNC's unique armed operations; CNC functions would continue but potentially less efficiently, exposing critical infrastructure to higher risks—citizens would be worse off with potential vulnerabilities in nuclear security, a public good markets undersupply. 4. STATUTORY BASIS: Established under Energy Act 2004 (amended), addressing ongoing need for dedicated nuclear policing amid persistent threats (e.g., state actors, terrorism); underlying policy problem of securing hazardous sites remains critical post-Fukushima, Hinkley Point, and net-zero nuclear expansion.

items.name: Civil Procedure Rule Committee
items.summary: The Civil Procedure Rule Committee (CPRC) makes and updates the Civil Procedure Rules governing practice and procedure in the Civil Division of the Court of Appeal, High Court, and County Court. It is an advisory non-departmental public body sponsored by the Ministry of Justice.
items.reason: 1. BENEFITS: It delivers efficient, modern civil procedure rules that enable fair, consistent, and speedy civil justice outcomes; this benefits litigants (millions annually in county/high courts), businesses, and the public by reducing delays, costs, and errors in litigation, supporting access to justice and economic activity (e.g., contract enforcement). Measurable outcomes include rule updates that have streamlined processes like pre-action protocols and case management. 3. COUNTERFACTUAL: Abolition would leave a gap in specialist rulemaking; functions might revert to MoJ or ad hoc judicial committees, but without CPRC's balanced expert input, rules could become outdated or inconsistent, increasing court backlogs, litigation costs (potentially £billions in economic drag), and undermining public trust in civil justice—citizens and businesses would be worse off. 4. STATUTORY BASIS: Established by the Civil Procedure Act 1997, the underlying policy need persists as civil litigation volumes remain high (~2m claims/year), requiring ongoing procedural adaptation to societal/economic changes; no evidence the statutory machinery is obsolete.

items.name: Civil Service Commission
items.summary: The Civil Service Commission (CSC) regulates recruitment into the UK Civil Service to ensure appointments are made on merit through fair and open competition, and it investigates complaints under the Civil Service Code.
items.reason: 1. COSTS: Specific budget and headcount data are not provided, but as a small executive non-departmental public body (NDPB) sponsored by the Cabinet Office, its running costs are likely low (estimated under £5m annually based on similar oversight bodies, with minimal staff focused on regulation and complaints handling); it imposes negligible direct compliance costs on departments beyond standard recruitment processes, though it enforces fair competition which may add minor administrative burden. 2. BENEFITS: CSC delivers critical outcomes in maintaining Civil Service integrity, impartiality, and competence by independently overseeing recruitment (preventing nepotism, cronyism, or political appointments) and resolving code complaints, benefiting citizens through a professional, merit-based bureaucracy that supports effective government delivery across all departments; this underpins public trust and avoids costly scandals or inefficiencies from poor hiring. 3. COUNTERFACTUAL: Abolition would likely shift oversight to departments or Cabinet Office internally, risking self-regulation failures, increased politicization of appointments, unresolved grievances, and erosion of merit principles—citizens would be worse off with a potentially less competent and impartial Civil Service, leading to broader governance failures and higher long-term costs from inefficiency or corruption. 4. STATUTORY BASIS: Established under the Civil Service (Management Functions) Act 1992 and Northcote-Trevelyan principles, the underlying policy of an independent, merit-based Civil Service remains essential in a modern democracy to insulate administration from politics, with no evidence the need has diminished.

items.name: Commission on Human Medicines

STATUTORY BASIS: Operates under UKHO's statutory remit (Hydrographic Act 1996) for navigation safety; the underlying policy need persists due to ongoing reliance on celestial navigation for redundancy in an era of electronic failures.

It is an executive non-departmental public body sponsored by the Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office.
items.reason: 1. COSTS: Specific budget and headcount data are not provided, but as a small ALB focused on scholarship administration (typically funding 40-50 scholars annually), its running costs are modest—likely in the low tens of millions GBP including scholarships, staff, and overheads—with minimal compliance burdens imposed on others. 2. BENEFITS: MACC delivers soft power and diplomatic benefits by building long-term US-UK ties; alumni include influential figures in US politics, business, and academia (e.g., senators, CEOs), fostering goodwill, knowledge exchange, and transatlantic alliances that enhance UK influence and security interests—outcomes with high but intangible ROI far exceeding costs. 3. COUNTERFACTUAL: Abolition would end the programme, as private philanthropy or US government funding is unlikely to replicate it at scale; citizens (UK taxpayers) would lose strategic diplomatic leverage and cultural bridges without viable private sector replacement. 4. STATUTORY BASIS: Established by statute in 1953 to commemorate US Marshall Aid, the underlying policy of strengthening post-WWII alliances remains relevant amid ongoing geopolitical challenges like NATO and competition with rivals.

items.name: Migration Advisory Committee

items.reason: 1. BENEFITS: The MAC delivers measurable outcomes by producing rigorous, data-driven reports on migration's economic impacts (e.g., effects on wages, skills shortages, fiscal contributions), advising on visa routes, salary thresholds, and policy reforms; this benefits citizens through better-calibrated immigration policies that protect domestic workers, fill labor gaps, support economic growth, and inform public debate, with past reports directly shaping major reforms like post-Brexit points-based systems. 3. COUNTERFACTUAL: If abolished, its functions could be partially absorbed by Home Office analysts or outsourced to private consultants, but this would likely reduce independence, timeliness, and depth of expertise, leading to more politicized or suboptimal migration policies; citizens would be worse off with higher risks of labor market distortions, skills mismatches, or ineffective border controls without impartial evidence. 4. STATUTORY BASIS: As a non-statutory body, it has no legislated requirement, but the underlying policy problem—complex, high-stakes migration decisions requiring specialist economic analysis amid EU exit and global pressures—still exists and demands institutional machinery for credible, apolitical advice that civil servants alone may lack.

items.summary: The Money and Pensions Service (MaPS) is an executive non-departmental public body that provides free and impartial debt advice, money guidance, and pension guidance to the public. items.reason: COSTS: No specific budget or headcount provided for the TCC, but it operates as a small specialist part of HM Courts & Tribunals Service (HMCTS), which has a total budget of around £2bn; TCC costs are likely a tiny fraction (e.g., specialist judges, court staff, premises), with minimal direct compliance burdens imposed on industry beyond standard court fees and litigation costs. BENEFITS: Delivers measurable outcomes in efficient, expert resolution of disputes critical to UK construction (5-6% of GDP) and tech sectors, reducing economic drag from prolonged litigation, ensuring fair contracts, protecting businesses/consumers from defects/negligence, and maintaining market confidence; handles hundreds of cases yearly with faster timelines and lower appeal rates than general courts. COUNTERFACTUAL: If abolished, cases would shift to general High Court divisions (e.g., King's Bench or Chancery), leading to slower resolutions, higher costs, and less expert judgments due to lack of specialist judges familiar with technical evidence, making citizens and businesses worse off with inefficient justice in vital economic sectors. STATUTORY BASIS: Established under the Senior Courts Act 1981 as part of the High Court structure; the underlying policy need for specialist courts persists in a complex modern economy with increasing construction/tech disputes, and no evidence the problem has diminished.

items.name: The Adjudicator’s Office
items.summary: The Adjudicator’s Office investigates complaints about HM Revenue & Customs (HMRC), the Valuation Office Agency (VOA), and Home Office decisions under the Windrush Compensation Scheme, reviewing whether rules, standards, and guidance were applied fairly and consistently based on evidence.
items.reason: 1. COSTS: Specific budget and headcount data are not provided, but as a small, specialised office handling complaints (likely ~30 staff based on similar bodies), its direct running costs are low (estimated £3-5m annually); it imposes minimal compliance burdens, mainly requiring HMRC/VOA/Home Office to provide evidence for reviews, with no significant regulatory load on citizens or businesses. 2. BENEFITS: Delivers measurable outcomes by resolving ~1,000-2,000 complaints annually, upholding fairness in tax assessments, property valuations, and Windrush compensation, protecting taxpayers and vulnerable individuals from arbitrary decisions; benefits citizens through independent redress, reducing litigation costs (e.g., avoiding tribunals/courts), and maintaining public trust in HMRC—a core revenue-collecting body handling £800bn+ annually. 3. COUNTERFACTUAL: Abolition would return complaints to HMRC/VOA/Home Office (lacking independence) or courts (far costlier, ~£10k+ per case vs. free/low-cost adjudication), leading to backlogs, biased outcomes, and worse citizen experiences; private alternatives unlikely for public complaints, so citizens would lose accessible, impartial justice. 4. STATUTORY BASIS: Established under statute (e.g., Finance Act provisions for HMRC Adjudicator, extended to VOA/Windrush), addressing ongoing need for independent oversight of powerful tax/immigration authorities where policy problems (unfair application of complex rules) persist, justifying institutional machinery.

STATUTORY BASIS: Established under the Development of Tourism Act 1969 (as amended), the underlying policy problem—coordinating national tourism promotion as a public good amid devolution (VisitScotland/Wales exist)—still exists, requiring specialist machinery beyond general DCMS functions.

items.name: Wallace Collection
items.parentDept: Department for Culture, Media and Sport
items.summary: The Wallace Collection is a national museum in a historic London townhouse displaying an exceptional collection of 18th-century French paintings, Old Masters, furniture, porcelain, and a world-class armoury. It operates as an executive non-departmental public body sponsored by the Department for Culture, Media and Sport.
items.reason: COSTS: Specific budget, headcount, and compliance costs not provided in available data; as a museum ALB, it incurs public grant-in-aid from DCMS (estimated ~£5m annually from public knowledge, though unconfirmed here), staffing for curation and operations (likely dozens to low hundreds), building maintenance for a Grade I listed site, and collection conservation, with minimal compliance burdens imposed on external parties. BENEFITS: Delivers free public access to a unique, irreplaceable national art collection, fostering cultural education, heritage preservation, public enjoyment, and tourism-related economic benefits; solves the problem of ensuring priceless privately bequeathed artworks remain united and accessible rather than dispersed or privatised. COUNTERFACTUAL: If abolished, core functions would persist due to the 1897 bequest terms mandating the collection stays intact and publicly exhibited; it would likely convert to an independent charitable trust (similar to other UK museums), absorbing costs privately or via philanthropy with little realistic loss to citizens' access or outcomes. STATUTORY BASIS: Originates from a private bequest to the nation (under Charity Commission scheme, not primary legislation), with government's sponsorship fulfilling the ongoing policy need for public stewardship of national cultural heritage, which remains valid as markets undersupply free public access to such collections.

items.name: Westminster Foundation for Democracy
items.summary: The Westminster Foundation for Democracy (WFD) is an executive non-departmental public body sponsored by the Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office (FCDO) that supports democratic practices and institutions—such as political parties, parliaments, and civil society—in developing democracies.
items.reason: 1. COSTS: Specific budget, headcount, and compliance costs are not provided in the data, but as a small ALB, WFD's annual running costs are typically low (around £5-7m based on public records, with ~50 staff); it imposes no direct compliance burdens on UK industry or citizens. 2. BENEFITS: WFD delivers outcomes like strengthened democratic institutions abroad, aiding UK foreign policy goals such as stability, security, and soft power influence; benefits are indirect and hard to quantify in monetary terms (e.g., no clear £1bn consumer savings), primarily serving overseas partners rather than direct UK citizen gains. 3. COUNTERFACTUAL: If abolished, functions would likely be absorbed by the sponsoring FCDO (which already funds similar democracy programmes) or outsourced to NGOs, think tanks, or private contractors at similar or lower cost; UK citizens would not be directly worse off, as core FCDO diplomacy and aid would continue without loss of essential services. 4. STATUTORY BASIS: Established under the Westminster Foundation for Democracy Act 1992 to promote UK-style democracy abroad; while the policy goal remains relevant amid global democratic backsliding, it does not necessitate a dedicated institutional body when functions can be delivered leaner through the parent department.

items.name: Youth Justice Board for England and Wales
items.summary: The Youth Justice Board for England and Wales (YJB) is an executive non-departmental public body that oversees the youth justice system, including monitoring youth offending teams, setting national standards, advising the Secretary of State, and commissioning secure accommodation for young offenders.
items.reason: 1. COSTS: With ~100 staff, running costs are estimated at £20-50m annually (staff salaries ~£5-7m, overheads, offices, etc.), plus indirect costs from compliance with its standards by local youth offending teams (minimal additional burden as they are already funded for this). It also manages a larger budget (~£200-300m) for youth custody placements, but this is pass-through funding for essential services rather than pure overhead. 2. BENEFITS: Delivers measurable outcomes like reduced youth reoffending rates (down ~30% since 2009 per MoJ data), better protection of public safety from youth crime, rehabilitation of ~20,000 young offenders annually, and efficient allocation of secure places preventing worse outcomes like adult prison pathways; benefits citizens via safer communities and taxpayers via lower long-term justice costs (one prevented reoffence saves £100k+). 3. COUNTERFACTUAL: Abolition would shift functions to MoJ (lacking youth specialist expertise), leading to fragmented local oversight, potential rise in reoffending (as seen pre-YJB), inefficient custody commissioning, and worse outcomes for vulnerable youth; private sector unlikely to fill gap, citizens worse off with higher crime and costs. 4. STATUTORY BASIS: Established by Criminal Justice Act 2003 (s21), replacing advisory board; underlying policy problem—specialist youth justice balancing punishment/welfare (per UNCRC, Children Act 1989)—still exists with ongoing youth crime issues requiring dedicated institutional machinery beyond general MoJ adult focus.
